Shrine buildings |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami
Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}} |Sacred object = |Rank = |Founded = |Main hall = |Other name = Okumiya (Inner Shrine) |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kattamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Aerial photo of Okama, the crater lake of Mt. Zaō in 1976.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|lt=1976|Q2480|WD=Q2480|1=1976}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 51) {{National Land Aerial Photography}}
At the center of the photo is {{ill|lt=Okama (Zao Mountains)|Q11489343|WD=Q11489343|lt=Okama (Goshiki-numa)|1=Okama Crater Lake}}, with Goshikidake to its east. These are surrounded by a “C”-shaped {{ill|lt=outer rim mountains|Q1491559|WD=Q1491559|1=Somma volcano}} open to the east. The shrine is located on the summit of Mt. Katta, part of the southern outer rim. On the summit of Mt. Kumano in the upper left of the photo is {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}}.]] '''Kattamine Shrine''' (かったみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the summit of Mt. Katta ({{ill|lt=elevation|Q2633778|WD=Q2633778|1=Elevation}} 1,758 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}), on the {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} side of the {{ill|lt=Zao Mountains|Q167951|WD=Q167951|1=Mount Zaō}} in the {{ill|lt=Ou Mountains|Q1357388|WD=Q1357388|1=Ōu Mountains}}. It forms a pair with {{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} at the foot of the mountain. This shrine is called the “'''Okumiya''' (Inner Shrine),” while the shrine of the same name in Togatta Onsen is called the “Satomiya (Village Shrine).” The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is seasonally {{ill|lt=relocated|Q327532|WD=Q327532|lt=enshrined|1=Shintai}} between the two shrines—at the summit Okumiya in summer and at the Satomiya at the foot of the mountain in winter. The name “Zao Mountains” derives from the {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} that were once worshipped at both shrines. === Location === It is said that the shrine was originally built atop a boundary mound between Ōshū and Ushū on Mt. Kumano, but because it was close to the crater and frequently burned due to eruptions, it was relocated around the {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} era (1645–1648) to the Omuro (Komedō) area of Mt. Katta after consultation between the Sendai Domain lord and the Matsudaira clan of Yamagata Zao Town History Compilation Committee, “Materials on Zao Mountain Worship,” *Zao Town History: Source Materials I*, 1987. Since ancient times, three shrines—Zao, {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|WD=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumano Shrine|Q3200355|WD=Q3200355|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano shrine}}—were enshrined on Mt. Kumano (elevation 1,841 m), the highest peak in the Zao range, and their village shrines still exist on both the Ōshū and Ushū sides. Along the main pilgrimage route (Miyagi side), the {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gates were erected as follows: the third torii at the Ōtorii enclosure in Maruta, Zao Town; the second torii at Iboishi; and the first torii at Fudō Falls. According to the documents of Sanjō-in at the Hōzawa route, the ritual method of worshipping the three shrines represents the correct ancient mountain practice . In {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 40), in the document “Shrine Survey Report” submitted by Kattamine Shrine (classified as a village shrine) included in *Survey of Shrines and Temples: Sendai City, Katta District, Shibata District, Volume 1 of 9* (held by the Miyagi Prefectural Archives), the shrine’s location is recorded as “enshrined at Kuraishi-dake, Miyamura, Katta District.” The report is dated July 4, Meiji 39, and lists Shrine Administrator Fujimoto Kunio and parish representatives from Miyamura, Katta District (present-day {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}), with some residing in Sendai City. On the other hand, the {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} Board of Education states that the shrine is located in {{ill|lt=Shichikashuku Town|Q1204398|WD=Q1204398|1=Shichikashuku, Miyagi}}, Katta District {{cite web|url=https://www.dokitan.com/column/02.html|title=Dokitan.com > History Column > No. 2 Kattamine Shrine (1)|publisher=Zao Town Board of Education|date=2009-05-03|accessdate=2025-07-06}}. === History === The shrine is identified as “Kattamine Shrine (Myōjin Taisha)” in the Katta District entry of the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}* (*Engishiki*, Volumes 9–10, {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5 [927]). The *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}* records its establishment in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901). In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 11 (869), the year of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} , the deity “Kattamine-no-Kami,” ranked Junior Ninth Rank, was promoted by extraordinary advancement to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. {{ill|lt=Sakuma Dōgan|Q6562843|WD=Q6562843|1=Sakuma Dōgan}} remarked “How sudden indeed,” suggesting that some records may be missing. {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|WD=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}} interpreted the Jōgan tsunami as a “divine calamity caused by volcanic awe,” viewing it as the “wrath of the mountain god” as understood by the imperial court. In contrast, the “Shrine Survey Report” cited above quotes from the *{{ill|lt=Ruijū Kokushi|Q1038639|WD=Q1038639|1=Ruijū Kokushi}}* (compiled {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 4 [892]), a rearrangement of the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, stating that the rank promotion was due to considerations of harvest conditions and drought or flood, without mentioning the tsunami. According to *Kinpu Sanzan Goengi*, the Zao Daigongen enshrined on Mt. Zao (Mt. Fubo) was opened by {{ill|lt=En no Ozunu|Q198946|WD=Q198946|1=En no Gyōja}} in {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 8 (679). It is also said to have been invited by his uncle Gangyō. The Zao Daigongen Shrine long supervised the ascetics of the Zao region, managed by Gankyō-ji Temple at the eastern foot of Ōkattayama (Aomayama). *Kinpusan Kai-en narabi ni Higashidake Onsen Raireki* records that it was later revived by the monk Jitsugan, a spiritual descendant of {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}}. By the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} (late {{ill|lt=12th century|Q7061|WD=Q7061|1=12th century}}), the shrine prospered under the patronage of the {{ill|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, with Gankyō-ji expanding to 48 sub-temples. After the clan’s fall, decline and destruction by warfare led to ruin, and following the {{ill|lt=Ōnin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}, only three temples remained: Sannobō, Miyamotobō, and Takenobō Tanabe Marefumi, *Fūnai Fudoki*, 1772. Sannobō later fell into ruin around the {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} era. Miyamotobō became Kyūrenzō-ji during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1573–1592), when Lord {{ill|lt=Date Masamune|Q311183|WD=Q311183|1=Date Masamune}} granted it the mountain and temple name “Hōchizan Renzō-ji” and donated 200 koku of land. By longstanding precedent, Renzō-ji assumed administrative control over the shrine. Takenobō, known as Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō, oversaw the main pilgrimage route. During the {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}} era of the {{ill|lt=Northern Court|Q1201819|WD=Q1201819|1=Northern Court}} (1368–1374), three Tendai ascetic offices—Sanjō-in, Anraku-in, and Kakuzembō—were established at different approaches, collectively called the Ushū Three Bettō, all operating under Takenobō’s direction. In the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, as mountain pilgrimage flourished, they guided many worshippers to the summit shrine, though this prosperity later led to disputes over rights, culminating in lawsuits during the {{ill|lt=Ka’ei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} era (1848–1855). The 1907 *Shrine Survey* notes that the place name “Kattadake” was of relatively modern origin, with the mountain historically called Fubōsan or Zaodake, also known as Kuraishidake. It explains that because the shrine was enshrined on the summit of Ōkattayama, the divine name “Kattamine Shrine” was adopted, though confusion over the name remains. With the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} and enforcement of the Shinto–Buddhist separation edicts, “Zao Gongen” was adopted as a Shinto divine name in Yoshino, and monks became Shinto priests. Following this, in July {{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720|1=1869}}, the name was changed from “Zao Daigongen” to “Zao Ōkami,” and the shrine was called “Zao Shrine.” In September the same year, based on the interpretation that “Zao Ōkami” referred to the two deities {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami and Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}}, the shrine was renamed “Mikumari Shrine.” By 1875, it came to be called “Kattamine Shrine.” === Seasonal Relocation === A map of the Togatta residence created by the Katakura family around the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} era does not show a “Zao Daigongen Temporary Shrine,” instead marking “Takenobō” at the current Satomiya location. *Ōu Kanshiki Monrōshi* (1719) records that a shrine of Zao Gongen stood on the summit, with torii gates along the eastern mountain paths. April 8 was called the “Opening,” when snow melted and roads opened, while October 8 marked the “Closing.” During winter, villagers held rites with steamed rice and cloudy sake. *An’ei Fudoki Goyōshodashi* (1777) records a “Zao Gongen Temporary Shrine,” noting that due to heavy snow, the deity was relocated downhill on October 8 and returned on April 8. The temporary shrine stood at the same location as Takenobō, indicating a close relationship. Today, relocation between the Satomiya and Okumiya coincides with the opening of the Zao Echo Line and {{ill|lt=Zao Highline|Q11621931|WD=Q11621931|1=Zao High Line}} roads, typically in late April, though timing varies by snowfall. Before relocation, {{ill|lt=Ōsaki Hachimangū|Q874428|WD=Q874428|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|WD=Q46747|1=Sendai}} performs annual snow-clearing service to expose the shrine buildings and approach paths. === Access === [[File:Kattamine shrine winter.jpg|thumb|The shrine during severe winter when roads are closed
(Photo taken March 9, 2006)]] {{seealso|Okama (Zao Mountains)}} From the free Zao Echo Line, enter the Zao Highline (daytime toll road), park at the free Katta Pass parking lot in front of the Miyagi Prefectural Zao Rest House, then walk about 15 minutes. For winter closures, see {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture Route / Yamagata Prefecture Route 12 Shiroishi–Kaminoyama Line#Zao Echo Line|Q11453957|WD=Q11453957|1=Miyagi Prefectural Road and Yamagata Prefectural Road Route 12}}. {{-}} === Shrines with the Same Name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Other name |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Pref.|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=District|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=Town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |Area !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} | |rowspan="2" |Zao / Mt. Katta (relocated from Mt. Kumano) |Summit area |{{Coord|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !Kattamine Shrine |'''Okumiya''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |Eastern foothills / former Takenobō site in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} |{{Coord|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aomayama|1=UNKNOWN}} |The mountain itself (Ōkattayama) enshrined together with Shiratori Shrine (Yamato Takeru) |{{Coord|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyōjin)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Miya, Zao Town)|Q11396254|WD=Q11396254|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Shiratori Daimyōjin''' |} {{Coordinate list|section=Shrines with the Same Name}} There are also shrines of the same name on the {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|WD=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} side: Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine, Shimohōzawa, {{ill|lt=Yamagata City|Q205526|WD=Q205526|1=Yamagata (city)}}), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hangō, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, {{ill|lt=Kaminoyama City|Q770187|WD=Q770187|1=Kaminoyama}}). === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}} === External links === * {{cite web|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622222024/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|archivedate=2019-06-22|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|title=Kattamine Shrine (Mutsu Province, Katta District)|publisher={{ill|lt=Kokugakuin University|Q495330|WD=Q495330|1=Kokugakuin University}}|accessdate=2025-07-06}} * [http://www.dokitan.com/index.html Official website “Dokitan.com” – History and Cultural Properties of Zao Town] (Zao Town Board of Education) * [http://www.zaodera.jp/ Kinpusan Zao-ji Temple] (A Shingon-affiliated independent temple sharing the historical name with the Edo-period “Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō,” with its main hall at Sai-no-Kawara along the Zao Echo Line and a branch temple in Togatta Onsen) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}} |Sacred object = |Rank = |Founded = |Main hall = |Other name = Okumiya (Inner Shrine) |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kattamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Aerial photo of Okama, the crater lake of Mt. Zaō in 1976.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|lt=1976|Q2480|WD=Q2480|1=1976}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 51) {{National Land Aerial Photography}}
At the center of the photo is {{ill|lt=Okama (Zao Mountains)|Q11489343|WD=Q11489343|lt=Okama (Goshiki-numa)|1=Okama Crater Lake}}, with Goshikidake to its east. These are surrounded by a “C”-shaped {{ill|lt=outer rim mountains|Q1491559|WD=Q1491559|1=Somma volcano}} open to the east. The shrine is located on the summit of Mt. Katta, part of the southern outer rim. On the summit of Mt. Kumano in the upper left of the photo is {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}}.]] '''Kattamine Shrine''' (かったみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the summit of Mt. Katta ({{ill|lt=elevation|Q2633778|WD=Q2633778|1=Elevation}} 1,758 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}), on the {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} side of the {{ill|lt=Zao Mountains|Q167951|WD=Q167951|1=Mount Zaō}} in the {{ill|lt=Ou Mountains|Q1357388|WD=Q1357388|1=Ōu Mountains}}. It forms a pair with {{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} at the foot of the mountain. This shrine is called the “'''Okumiya''' (Inner Shrine),” while the shrine of the same name in Togatta Onsen is called the “Satomiya (Village Shrine).” The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is seasonally {{ill|lt=relocated|Q327532|WD=Q327532|lt=enshrined|1=Shintai}} between the two shrines—at the summit Okumiya in summer and at the Satomiya at the foot of the mountain in winter. The name “Zao Mountains” derives from the {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} that were once worshipped at both shrines. === Location === It is said that the shrine was originally built atop a boundary mound between Ōshū and Ushū on Mt. Kumano, but because it was close to the crater and frequently burned due to eruptions, it was relocated around the {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} era (1645–1648) to the Omuro (Komedō) area of Mt. Katta after consultation between the Sendai Domain lord and the Matsudaira clan of Yamagata Zao Town History Compilation Committee, “Materials on Zao Mountain Worship,” *Zao Town History: Source Materials I*, 1987. Since ancient times, three shrines—Zao, {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|WD=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumano Shrine|Q3200355|WD=Q3200355|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano shrine}}—were enshrined on Mt. Kumano (elevation 1,841 m), the highest peak in the Zao range, and their village shrines still exist on both the Ōshū and Ushū sides. Along the main pilgrimage route (Miyagi side), the {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gates were erected as follows: the third torii at the Ōtorii enclosure in Maruta, Zao Town; the second torii at Iboishi; and the first torii at Fudō Falls. According to the documents of Sanjō-in at the Hōzawa route, the ritual method of worshipping the three shrines represents the correct ancient mountain practice . In {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 40), in the document “Shrine Survey Report” submitted by Kattamine Shrine (classified as a village shrine) included in *Survey of Shrines and Temples: Sendai City, Katta District, Shibata District, Volume 1 of 9* (held by the Miyagi Prefectural Archives), the shrine’s location is recorded as “enshrined at Kuraishi-dake, Miyamura, Katta District.” The report is dated July 4, Meiji 39, and lists Shrine Administrator Fujimoto Kunio and parish representatives from Miyamura, Katta District (present-day {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}), with some residing in Sendai City. On the other hand, the {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} Board of Education states that the shrine is located in {{ill|lt=Shichikashuku Town|Q1204398|WD=Q1204398|1=Shichikashuku, Miyagi}}, Katta District {{cite web|url=https://www.dokitan.com/column/02.html|title=Dokitan.com > History Column > No. 2 Kattamine Shrine (1)|publisher=Zao Town Board of Education|date=2009-05-03|accessdate=2025-07-06}}. === History === The shrine is identified as “Kattamine Shrine (Myōjin Taisha)” in the Katta District entry of the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}* (*Engishiki*, Volumes 9–10, {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5 [927]). The *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}* records its establishment in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901). In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 11 (869), the year of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} , the deity “Kattamine-no-Kami,” ranked Junior Ninth Rank, was promoted by extraordinary advancement to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. {{ill|lt=Sakuma Dōgan|Q6562843|WD=Q6562843|1=Sakuma Dōgan}} remarked “How sudden indeed,” suggesting that some records may be missing. {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|WD=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}} interpreted the Jōgan tsunami as a “divine calamity caused by volcanic awe,” viewing it as the “wrath of the mountain god” as understood by the imperial court. In contrast, the “Shrine Survey Report” cited above quotes from the *{{ill|lt=Ruijū Kokushi|Q1038639|WD=Q1038639|1=Ruijū Kokushi}}* (compiled {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 4 [892]), a rearrangement of the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, stating that the rank promotion was due to considerations of harvest conditions and drought or flood, without mentioning the tsunami. According to *Kinpu Sanzan Goengi*, the Zao Daigongen enshrined on Mt. Zao (Mt. Fubo) was opened by {{ill|lt=En no Ozunu|Q198946|WD=Q198946|1=En no Gyōja}} in {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 8 (679). It is also said to have been invited by his uncle Gangyō. The Zao Daigongen Shrine long supervised the ascetics of the Zao region, managed by Gankyō-ji Temple at the eastern foot of Ōkattayama (Aomayama). *Kinpusan Kai-en narabi ni Higashidake Onsen Raireki* records that it was later revived by the monk Jitsugan, a spiritual descendant of {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}}. By the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} (late {{ill|lt=12th century|Q7061|WD=Q7061|1=12th century}}), the shrine prospered under the patronage of the {{ill|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, with Gankyō-ji expanding to 48 sub-temples. After the clan’s fall, decline and destruction by warfare led to ruin, and following the {{ill|lt=Ōnin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}, only three temples remained: Sannobō, Miyamotobō, and Takenobō Tanabe Marefumi, *Fūnai Fudoki*, 1772. Sannobō later fell into ruin around the {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} era. Miyamotobō became Kyūrenzō-ji during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1573–1592), when Lord {{ill|lt=Date Masamune|Q311183|WD=Q311183|1=Date Masamune}} granted it the mountain and temple name “Hōchizan Renzō-ji” and donated 200 koku of land. By longstanding precedent, Renzō-ji assumed administrative control over the shrine. Takenobō, known as Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō, oversaw the main pilgrimage route. During the {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}} era of the {{ill|lt=Northern Court|Q1201819|WD=Q1201819|1=Northern Court}} (1368–1374), three Tendai ascetic offices—Sanjō-in, Anraku-in, and Kakuzembō—were established at different approaches, collectively called the Ushū Three Bettō, all operating under Takenobō’s direction. In the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, as mountain pilgrimage flourished, they guided many worshippers to the summit shrine, though this prosperity later led to disputes over rights, culminating in lawsuits during the {{ill|lt=Ka’ei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} era (1848–1855). The 1907 *Shrine Survey* notes that the place name “Kattadake” was of relatively modern origin, with the mountain historically called Fubōsan or Zaodake, also known as Kuraishidake. It explains that because the shrine was enshrined on the summit of Ōkattayama, the divine name “Kattamine Shrine” was adopted, though confusion over the name remains. With the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} and enforcement of the Shinto–Buddhist separation edicts, “Zao Gongen” was adopted as a Shinto divine name in Yoshino, and monks became Shinto priests. Following this, in July {{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720|1=1869}}, the name was changed from “Zao Daigongen” to “Zao Ōkami,” and the shrine was called “Zao Shrine.” In September the same year, based on the interpretation that “Zao Ōkami” referred to the two deities {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami and Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}}, the shrine was renamed “Mikumari Shrine.” By 1875, it came to be called “Kattamine Shrine.” === Seasonal Relocation === A map of the Togatta residence created by the Katakura family around the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} era does not show a “Zao Daigongen Temporary Shrine,” instead marking “Takenobō” at the current Satomiya location. *Ōu Kanshiki Monrōshi* (1719) records that a shrine of Zao Gongen stood on the summit, with torii gates along the eastern mountain paths. April 8 was called the “Opening,” when snow melted and roads opened, while October 8 marked the “Closing.” During winter, villagers held rites with steamed rice and cloudy sake. *An’ei Fudoki Goyōshodashi* (1777) records a “Zao Gongen Temporary Shrine,” noting that due to heavy snow, the deity was relocated downhill on October 8 and returned on April 8. The temporary shrine stood at the same location as Takenobō, indicating a close relationship. Today, relocation between the Satomiya and Okumiya coincides with the opening of the Zao Echo Line and {{ill|lt=Zao Highline|Q11621931|WD=Q11621931|1=Zao High Line}} roads, typically in late April, though timing varies by snowfall. Before relocation, {{ill|lt=Ōsaki Hachimangū|Q874428|WD=Q874428|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|WD=Q46747|1=Sendai}} performs annual snow-clearing service to expose the shrine buildings and approach paths. === Access === [[File:Kattamine shrine winter.jpg|thumb|The shrine during severe winter when roads are closed
(Photo taken March 9, 2006)]] {{seealso|Okama (Zao Mountains)}} From the free Zao Echo Line, enter the Zao Highline (daytime toll road), park at the free Katta Pass parking lot in front of the Miyagi Prefectural Zao Rest House, then walk about 15 minutes. For winter closures, see {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture Route / Yamagata Prefecture Route 12 Shiroishi–Kaminoyama Line#Zao Echo Line|Q11453957|WD=Q11453957|1=Miyagi Prefectural Road and Yamagata Prefectural Road Route 12}}. {{-}} === Shrines with the Same Name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Other name |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Pref.|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=District|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=Town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |Area !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} | |rowspan="2" |Zao / Mt. Katta (relocated from Mt. Kumano) |Summit area |{{Coord|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !Kattamine Shrine |'''Okumiya''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |Eastern foothills / former Takenobō site in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} |{{Coord|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aomayama|1=UNKNOWN}} |The mountain itself (Ōkattayama) enshrined together with Shiratori Shrine (Yamato Takeru) |{{Coord|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyōjin)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Miya, Zao Town)|Q11396254|WD=Q11396254|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Shiratori Daimyōjin''' |} {{Coordinate list|section=Shrines with the Same Name}} There are also shrines of the same name on the {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|WD=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} side: Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine, Shimohōzawa, {{ill|lt=Yamagata City|Q205526|WD=Q205526|1=Yamagata (city)}}), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hangō, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, {{ill|lt=Kaminoyama City|Q770187|WD=Q770187|1=Kaminoyama}}). === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}} === External links === * {{cite web|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622222024/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|archivedate=2019-06-22|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|title=Kattamine Shrine (Mutsu Province, Katta District)|publisher={{ill|lt=Kokugakuin University|Q495330|WD=Q495330|1=Kokugakuin University}}|accessdate=2025-07-06}} * [http://www.dokitan.com/index.html Official website “Dokitan.com” – History and Cultural Properties of Zao Town] (Zao Town Board of Education) * [http://www.zaodera.jp/ Kinpusan Zao-ji Temple] (A Shingon-affiliated independent temple sharing the historical name with the Edo-period “Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō,” with its main hall at Sai-no-Kawara along the Zao Echo Line and a branch temple in Togatta Onsen) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Zaō in 1976.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|lt=1976|Q2480|WD=Q2480|1=1976}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 51) {{National Land Aerial Photography}}
At the center of the photo is {{ill|lt=Okama (Zao Mountains)|Q11489343|WD=Q11489343|lt=Okama (Goshiki-numa)|1=Okama Crater Lake}}, with Goshikidake to its east. These are surrounded by a “C”-shaped {{ill|lt=outer rim mountains|Q1491559|WD=Q1491559|1=Somma volcano}} open to the east. The shrine is located on the summit of Mt. Katta, part of the southern outer rim. On the summit of Mt. Kumano in the upper left of the photo is {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}}.]] '''Kattamine Shrine''' (かったみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the summit of Mt. Katta ({{ill|lt=elevation|Q2633778|WD=Q2633778|1=Elevation}} 1,758 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}), on the {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} side of the {{ill|lt=Zao Mountains|Q167951|WD=Q167951|1=Mount Zaō}} in the {{ill|lt=Ou Mountains|Q1357388|WD=Q1357388|1=Ōu Mountains}}. It forms a pair with {{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} at the foot of the mountain. This shrine is called the “'''Okumiya''' (Inner Shrine),” while the shrine of the same name in Togatta Onsen is called the “Satomiya (Village Shrine).” The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is seasonally {{ill|lt=relocated|Q327532|WD=Q327532|lt=enshrined|1=Shintai}} between the two shrines—at the summit Okumiya in summer and at the Satomiya at the foot of the mountain in winter. The name “Zao Mountains” derives from the {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} that were once worshipped at both shrines. === Location === It is said that the shrine was originally built atop a boundary mound between Ōshū and Ushū on Mt. Kumano, but because it was close to the crater and frequently burned due to eruptions, it was relocated around the {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} era (1645–1648) to the Omuro (Komedō) area of Mt. Katta after consultation between the Sendai Domain lord and the Matsudaira clan of Yamagata Zao Town History Compilation Committee, “Materials on Zao Mountain Worship,” *Zao Town History: Source Materials I*, 1987. Since ancient times, three shrines—Zao, {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|WD=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumano Shrine|Q3200355|WD=Q3200355|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano shrine}}—were enshrined on Mt. Kumano (elevation 1,841 m), the highest peak in the Zao range, and their village shrines still exist on both the Ōshū and Ushū sides. Along the main pilgrimage route (Miyagi side), the {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gates were erected as follows: the third torii at the Ōtorii enclosure in Maruta, Zao Town; the second torii at Iboishi; and the first torii at Fudō Falls. According to the documents of Sanjō-in at the Hōzawa route, the ritual method of worshipping the three shrines represents the correct ancient mountain practice . In {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 40), in the document “Shrine Survey Report” submitted by Kattamine Shrine (classified as a village shrine) included in *Survey of Shrines and Temples: Sendai City, Katta District, Shibata District, Volume 1 of 9* (held by the Miyagi Prefectural Archives), the shrine’s location is recorded as “enshrined at Kuraishi-dake, Miyamura, Katta District.” The report is dated July 4, Meiji 39, and lists Shrine Administrator Fujimoto Kunio and parish representatives from Miyamura, Katta District (present-day {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}), with some residing in Sendai City. On the other hand, the {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} Board of Education states that the shrine is located in {{ill|lt=Shichikashuku Town|Q1204398|WD=Q1204398|1=Shichikashuku, Miyagi}}, Katta District {{cite web|url=https://www.dokitan.com/column/02.html|title=Dokitan.com > History Column > No. 2 Kattamine Shrine (1)|publisher=Zao Town Board of Education|date=2009-05-03|accessdate=2025-07-06}}. === History === The shrine is identified as “Kattamine Shrine (Myōjin Taisha)” in the Katta District entry of the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}* (*Engishiki*, Volumes 9–10, {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5 [927]). The *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}* records its establishment in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901). In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 11 (869), the year of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} , the deity “Kattamine-no-Kami,” ranked Junior Ninth Rank, was promoted by extraordinary advancement to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. {{ill|lt=Sakuma Dōgan|Q6562843|WD=Q6562843|1=Sakuma Dōgan}} remarked “How sudden indeed,” suggesting that some records may be missing. {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|WD=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}} interpreted the Jōgan tsunami as a “divine calamity caused by volcanic awe,” viewing it as the “wrath of the mountain god” as understood by the imperial court. In contrast, the “Shrine Survey Report” cited above quotes from the *{{ill|lt=Ruijū Kokushi|Q1038639|WD=Q1038639|1=Ruijū Kokushi}}* (compiled {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 4 [892]), a rearrangement of the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, stating that the rank promotion was due to considerations of harvest conditions and drought or flood, without mentioning the tsunami. According to *Kinpu Sanzan Goengi*, the Zao Daigongen enshrined on Mt. Zao (Mt. Fubo) was opened by {{ill|lt=En no Ozunu|Q198946|WD=Q198946|1=En no Gyōja}} in {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 8 (679). It is also said to have been invited by his uncle Gangyō. The Zao Daigongen Shrine long supervised the ascetics of the Zao region, managed by Gankyō-ji Temple at the eastern foot of Ōkattayama (Aomayama). *Kinpusan Kai-en narabi ni Higashidake Onsen Raireki* records that it was later revived by the monk Jitsugan, a spiritual descendant of {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}}. By the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} (late {{ill|lt=12th century|Q7061|WD=Q7061|1=12th century}}), the shrine prospered under the patronage of the {{ill|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, with Gankyō-ji expanding to 48 sub-temples. After the clan’s fall, decline and destruction by warfare led to ruin, and following the {{ill|lt=Ōnin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}, only three temples remained: Sannobō, Miyamotobō, and Takenobō Tanabe Marefumi, *Fūnai Fudoki*, 1772. Sannobō later fell into ruin around the {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} era. Miyamotobō became Kyūrenzō-ji during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1573–1592), when Lord {{ill|lt=Date Masamune|Q311183|WD=Q311183|1=Date Masamune}} granted it the mountain and temple name “Hōchizan Renzō-ji” and donated 200 koku of land. By longstanding precedent, Renzō-ji assumed administrative control over the shrine. Takenobō, known as Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō, oversaw the main pilgrimage route. During the {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}} era of the {{ill|lt=Northern Court|Q1201819|WD=Q1201819|1=Northern Court}} (1368–1374), three Tendai ascetic offices—Sanjō-in, Anraku-in, and Kakuzembō—were established at different approaches, collectively called the Ushū Three Bettō, all operating under Takenobō’s direction. In the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, as mountain pilgrimage flourished, they guided many worshippers to the summit shrine, though this prosperity later led to disputes over rights, culminating in lawsuits during the {{ill|lt=Ka’ei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} era (1848–1855). The 1907 *Shrine Survey* notes that the place name “Kattadake” was of relatively modern origin, with the mountain historically called Fubōsan or Zaodake, also known as Kuraishidake. It explains that because the shrine was enshrined on the summit of Ōkattayama, the divine name “Kattamine Shrine” was adopted, though confusion over the name remains. With the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} and enforcement of the Shinto–Buddhist separation edicts, “Zao Gongen” was adopted as a Shinto divine name in Yoshino, and monks became Shinto priests. Following this, in July {{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720|1=1869}}, the name was changed from “Zao Daigongen” to “Zao Ōkami,” and the shrine was called “Zao Shrine.” In September the same year, based on the interpretation that “Zao Ōkami” referred to the two deities {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami and Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}}, the shrine was renamed “Mikumari Shrine.” By 1875, it came to be called “Kattamine Shrine.” === Seasonal Relocation === A map of the Togatta residence created by the Katakura family around the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} era does not show a “Zao Daigongen Temporary Shrine,” instead marking “Takenobō” at the current Satomiya location. *Ōu Kanshiki Monrōshi* (1719) records that a shrine of Zao Gongen stood on the summit, with torii gates along the eastern mountain paths. April 8 was called the “Opening,” when snow melted and roads opened, while October 8 marked the “Closing.” During winter, villagers held rites with steamed rice and cloudy sake. *An’ei Fudoki Goyōshodashi* (1777) records a “Zao Gongen Temporary Shrine,” noting that due to heavy snow, the deity was relocated downhill on October 8 and returned on April 8. The temporary shrine stood at the same location as Takenobō, indicating a close relationship. Today, relocation between the Satomiya and Okumiya coincides with the opening of the Zao Echo Line and {{ill|lt=Zao Highline|Q11621931|WD=Q11621931|1=Zao High Line}} roads, typically in late April, though timing varies by snowfall. Before relocation, {{ill|lt=Ōsaki Hachimangū|Q874428|WD=Q874428|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|WD=Q46747|1=Sendai}} performs annual snow-clearing service to expose the shrine buildings and approach paths. === Access === [[File:Kattamine shrine winter.jpg|thumb|The shrine during severe winter when roads are closed
(Photo taken March 9, 2006)]] {{seealso|Okama (Zao Mountains)}} From the free Zao Echo Line, enter the Zao Highline (daytime toll road), park at the free Katta Pass parking lot in front of the Miyagi Prefectural Zao Rest House, then walk about 15 minutes. For winter closures, see {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture Route / Yamagata Prefecture Route 12 Shiroishi–Kaminoyama Line#Zao Echo Line|Q11453957|WD=Q11453957|1=Miyagi Prefectural Road and Yamagata Prefectural Road Route 12}}. {{-}} === Shrines with the Same Name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Other name |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Pref.|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=District|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=Town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |Area !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} | |rowspan="2" |Zao / Mt. Katta (relocated from Mt. Kumano) |Summit area |{{Coord|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !Kattamine Shrine |'''Okumiya''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |Eastern foothills / former Takenobō site in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} |{{Coord|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aomayama|1=UNKNOWN}} |The mountain itself (Ōkattayama) enshrined together with Shiratori Shrine (Yamato Takeru) |{{Coord|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyōjin)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Miya, Zao Town)|Q11396254|WD=Q11396254|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Shiratori Daimyōjin''' |} {{Coordinate list|section=Shrines with the Same Name}} There are also shrines of the same name on the {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|WD=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} side: Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine, Shimohōzawa, {{ill|lt=Yamagata City|Q205526|WD=Q205526|1=Yamagata (city)}}), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hangō, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, {{ill|lt=Kaminoyama City|Q770187|WD=Q770187|1=Kaminoyama}}). === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}} === External links === * {{cite web|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622222024/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|archivedate=2019-06-22|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|title=Kattamine Shrine (Mutsu Province, Katta District)|publisher={{ill|lt=Kokugakuin University|Q495330|WD=Q495330|1=Kokugakuin University}}|accessdate=2025-07-06}} * [http://www.dokitan.com/index.html Official website “Dokitan.com” – History and Cultural Properties of Zao Town] (Zao Town Board of Education) * [http://www.zaodera.jp/ Kinpusan Zao-ji Temple] (A Shingon-affiliated independent temple sharing the historical name with the Edo-period “Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō,” with its main hall at Sai-no-Kawara along the Zao Echo Line and a branch temple in Togatta Onsen) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Shrine buildings |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami
Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}} |Sacred object = |Rank = |Founded = |Main hall = |Other name = Okumiya (Inner Shrine) |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kattamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Aerial photo of Okama, the crater lake of Mt. Zaō in 1976.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|lt=1976|Q2480|WD=Q2480|1=1976}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 51) {{National Land Aerial Photography}}
At the center of the photo is {{ill|lt=Okama (Zao Mountains)|Q11489343|WD=Q11489343|lt=Okama (Goshiki-numa)|1=Okama Crater Lake}}, with Goshikidake to its east. These are surrounded by a “C”-shaped {{ill|lt=outer rim mountains|Q1491559|WD=Q1491559|1=Somma volcano}} open to the east. The shrine is located on the summit of Mt. Katta, part of the southern outer rim. On the summit of Mt. Kumano in the upper left of the photo is {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}}.]] '''Kattamine Shrine''' (かったみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the summit of Mt. Katta ({{ill|lt=elevation|Q2633778|WD=Q2633778|1=Elevation}} 1,758 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}), on the {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} side of the {{ill|lt=Zao Mountains|Q167951|WD=Q167951|1=Mount Zaō}} in the {{ill|lt=Ou Mountains|Q1357388|WD=Q1357388|1=Ōu Mountains}}. It forms a pair with {{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} at the foot of the mountain. This shrine is called the “'''Okumiya''' (Inner Shrine),” while the shrine of the same name in Togatta Onsen is called the “Satomiya (Village Shrine).” The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is seasonally {{ill|lt=relocated|Q327532|WD=Q327532|lt=enshrined|1=Shintai}} between the two shrines—at the summit Okumiya in summer and at the Satomiya at the foot of the mountain in winter. The name “Zao Mountains” derives from the {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} that were once worshipped at both shrines. === Location === It is said that the shrine was originally built atop a boundary mound between Ōshū and Ushū on Mt. Kumano, but because it was close to the crater and frequently burned due to eruptions, it was relocated around the {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} era (1645–1648) to the Omuro (Komedō) area of Mt. Katta after consultation between the Sendai Domain lord and the Matsudaira clan of Yamagata Zao Town History Compilation Committee, “Materials on Zao Mountain Worship,” *Zao Town History: Source Materials I*, 1987. Since ancient times, three shrines—Zao, {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|WD=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumano Shrine|Q3200355|WD=Q3200355|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano shrine}}—were enshrined on Mt. Kumano (elevation 1,841 m), the highest peak in the Zao range, and their village shrines still exist on both the Ōshū and Ushū sides. Along the main pilgrimage route (Miyagi side), the {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gates were erected as follows: the third torii at the Ōtorii enclosure in Maruta, Zao Town; the second torii at Iboishi; and the first torii at Fudō Falls. According to the documents of Sanjō-in at the Hōzawa route, the ritual method of worshipping the three shrines represents the correct ancient mountain practice . In {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 40), in the document “Shrine Survey Report” submitted by Kattamine Shrine (classified as a village shrine) included in *Survey of Shrines and Temples: Sendai City, Katta District, Shibata District, Volume 1 of 9* (held by the Miyagi Prefectural Archives), the shrine’s location is recorded as “enshrined at Kuraishi-dake, Miyamura, Katta District.” The report is dated July 4, Meiji 39, and lists Shrine Administrator Fujimoto Kunio and parish representatives from Miyamura, Katta District (present-day {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}), with some residing in Sendai City. On the other hand, the {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} Board of Education states that the shrine is located in {{ill|lt=Shichikashuku Town|Q1204398|WD=Q1204398|1=Shichikashuku, Miyagi}}, Katta District {{cite web|url=https://www.dokitan.com/column/02.html|title=Dokitan.com > History Column > No. 2 Kattamine Shrine (1)|publisher=Zao Town Board of Education|date=2009-05-03|accessdate=2025-07-06}}. === History === The shrine is identified as “Kattamine Shrine (Myōjin Taisha)” in the Katta District entry of the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}* (*Engishiki*, Volumes 9–10, {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5 [927]). The *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}* records its establishment in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901). In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 11 (869), the year of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} , the deity “Kattamine-no-Kami,” ranked Junior Ninth Rank, was promoted by extraordinary advancement to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. {{ill|lt=Sakuma Dōgan|Q6562843|WD=Q6562843|1=Sakuma Dōgan}} remarked “How sudden indeed,” suggesting that some records may be missing. {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|WD=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}} interpreted the Jōgan tsunami as a “divine calamity caused by volcanic awe,” viewing it as the “wrath of the mountain god” as understood by the imperial court. In contrast, the “Shrine Survey Report” cited above quotes from the *{{ill|lt=Ruijū Kokushi|Q1038639|WD=Q1038639|1=Ruijū Kokushi}}* (compiled {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 4 [892]), a rearrangement of the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, stating that the rank promotion was due to considerations of harvest conditions and drought or flood, without mentioning the tsunami. According to *Kinpu Sanzan Goengi*, the Zao Daigongen enshrined on Mt. Zao (Mt. Fubo) was opened by {{ill|lt=En no Ozunu|Q198946|WD=Q198946|1=En no Gyōja}} in {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 8 (679). It is also said to have been invited by his uncle Gangyō. The Zao Daigongen Shrine long supervised the ascetics of the Zao region, managed by Gankyō-ji Temple at the eastern foot of Ōkattayama (Aomayama). *Kinpusan Kai-en narabi ni Higashidake Onsen Raireki* records that it was later revived by the monk Jitsugan, a spiritual descendant of {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}}. By the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} (late {{ill|lt=12th century|Q7061|WD=Q7061|1=12th century}}), the shrine prospered under the patronage of the {{ill|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, with Gankyō-ji expanding to 48 sub-temples. After the clan’s fall, decline and destruction by warfare led to ruin, and following the {{ill|lt=Ōnin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}, only three temples remained: Sannobō, Miyamotobō, and Takenobō Tanabe Marefumi, *Fūnai Fudoki*, 1772. Sannobō later fell into ruin around the {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} era. Miyamotobō became Kyūrenzō-ji during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1573–1592), when Lord {{ill|lt=Date Masamune|Q311183|WD=Q311183|1=Date Masamune}} granted it the mountain and temple name “Hōchizan Renzō-ji” and donated 200 koku of land. By longstanding precedent, Renzō-ji assumed administrative control over the shrine. Takenobō, known as Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō, oversaw the main pilgrimage route. During the {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}} era of the {{ill|lt=Northern Court|Q1201819|WD=Q1201819|1=Northern Court}} (1368–1374), three Tendai ascetic offices—Sanjō-in, Anraku-in, and Kakuzembō—were established at different approaches, collectively called the Ushū Three Bettō, all operating under Takenobō’s direction. In the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, as mountain pilgrimage flourished, they guided many worshippers to the summit shrine, though this prosperity later led to disputes over rights, culminating in lawsuits during the {{ill|lt=Ka’ei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} era (1848–1855). The 1907 *Shrine Survey* notes that the place name “Kattadake” was of relatively modern origin, with the mountain historically called Fubōsan or Zaodake, also known as Kuraishidake. It explains that because the shrine was enshrined on the summit of Ōkattayama, the divine name “Kattamine Shrine” was adopted, though confusion over the name remains. With the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} and enforcement of the Shinto–Buddhist separation edicts, “Zao Gongen” was adopted as a Shinto divine name in Yoshino, and monks became Shinto priests. Following this, in July {{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720|1=1869}}, the name was changed from “Zao Daigongen” to “Zao Ōkami,” and the shrine was called “Zao Shrine.” In September the same year, based on the interpretation that “Zao Ōkami” referred to the two deities {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami and Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}}, the shrine was renamed “Mikumari Shrine.” By 1875, it came to be called “Kattamine Shrine.” === Seasonal Relocation === A map of the Togatta residence created by the Katakura family around the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} era does not show a “Zao Daigongen Temporary Shrine,” instead marking “Takenobō” at the current Satomiya location. *Ōu Kanshiki Monrōshi* (1719) records that a shrine of Zao Gongen stood on the summit, with torii gates along the eastern mountain paths. April 8 was called the “Opening,” when snow melted and roads opened, while October 8 marked the “Closing.” During winter, villagers held rites with steamed rice and cloudy sake. *An’ei Fudoki Goyōshodashi* (1777) records a “Zao Gongen Temporary Shrine,” noting that due to heavy snow, the deity was relocated downhill on October 8 and returned on April 8. The temporary shrine stood at the same location as Takenobō, indicating a close relationship. Today, relocation between the Satomiya and Okumiya coincides with the opening of the Zao Echo Line and {{ill|lt=Zao Highline|Q11621931|WD=Q11621931|1=Zao High Line}} roads, typically in late April, though timing varies by snowfall. Before relocation, {{ill|lt=Ōsaki Hachimangū|Q874428|WD=Q874428|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|WD=Q46747|1=Sendai}} performs annual snow-clearing service to expose the shrine buildings and approach paths. === Access === [[File:Kattamine shrine winter.jpg|thumb|The shrine during severe winter when roads are closed
(Photo taken March 9, 2006)]] {{seealso|Okama (Zao Mountains)}} From the free Zao Echo Line, enter the Zao Highline (daytime toll road), park at the free Katta Pass parking lot in front of the Miyagi Prefectural Zao Rest House, then walk about 15 minutes. For winter closures, see {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture Route / Yamagata Prefecture Route 12 Shiroishi–Kaminoyama Line#Zao Echo Line|Q11453957|WD=Q11453957|1=Miyagi Prefectural Road and Yamagata Prefectural Road Route 12}}. {{-}} === Shrines with the Same Name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Other name |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Pref.|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=District|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=Town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |Area !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} | |rowspan="2" |Zao / Mt. Katta (relocated from Mt. Kumano) |Summit area |{{Coord|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !Kattamine Shrine |'''Okumiya''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |Eastern foothills / former Takenobō site in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} |{{Coord|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aomayama|1=UNKNOWN}} |The mountain itself (Ōkattayama) enshrined together with Shiratori Shrine (Yamato Takeru) |{{Coord|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyōjin)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Miya, Zao Town)|Q11396254|WD=Q11396254|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Shiratori Daimyōjin''' |} {{Coordinate list|section=Shrines with the Same Name}} There are also shrines of the same name on the {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|WD=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} side: Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine, Shimohōzawa, {{ill|lt=Yamagata City|Q205526|WD=Q205526|1=Yamagata (city)}}), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hangō, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, {{ill|lt=Kaminoyama City|Q770187|WD=Q770187|1=Kaminoyama}}). === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}} === External links === * {{cite web|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622222024/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|archivedate=2019-06-22|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|title=Kattamine Shrine (Mutsu Province, Katta District)|publisher={{ill|lt=Kokugakuin University|Q495330|WD=Q495330|1=Kokugakuin University}}|accessdate=2025-07-06}} * [http://www.dokitan.com/index.html Official website “Dokitan.com” – History and Cultural Properties of Zao Town] (Zao Town Board of Education) * [http://www.zaodera.jp/ Kinpusan Zao-ji Temple] (A Shingon-affiliated independent temple sharing the historical name with the Edo-period “Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō,” with its main hall at Sai-no-Kawara along the Zao Echo Line and a branch temple in Togatta Onsen) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Shrine buildings |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami
Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}} |Sacred object = |Rank = |Founded = |Main hall = |Other name = Okumiya (Inner Shrine) |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kattamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Aerial photo of Okama, the crater lake of Mt. Zaō in 1976.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|lt=1976|Q2480|WD=Q2480|1=1976}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 51) {{National Land Aerial Photography}}
At the center of the photo is {{ill|lt=Okama (Zao Mountains)|Q11489343|WD=Q11489343|lt=Okama (Goshiki-numa)|1=Okama Crater Lake}}, with Goshikidake to its east. These are surrounded by a “C”-shaped {{ill|lt=outer rim mountains|Q1491559|WD=Q1491559|1=Somma volcano}} open to the east. The shrine is located on the summit of Mt. Katta, part of the southern outer rim. On the summit of Mt. Kumano in the upper left of the photo is {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}}.]] '''Kattamine Shrine''' (かったみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located on the summit of Mt. Katta ({{ill|lt=elevation|Q2633778|WD=Q2633778|1=Elevation}} 1,758 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}), on the {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} side of the {{ill|lt=Zao Mountains|Q167951|WD=Q167951|1=Mount Zaō}} in the {{ill|lt=Ou Mountains|Q1357388|WD=Q1357388|1=Ōu Mountains}}. It forms a pair with {{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} at the foot of the mountain. This shrine is called the “'''Okumiya''' (Inner Shrine),” while the shrine of the same name in Togatta Onsen is called the “Satomiya (Village Shrine).” The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is seasonally {{ill|lt=relocated|Q327532|WD=Q327532|lt=enshrined|1=Shintai}} between the two shrines—at the summit Okumiya in summer and at the Satomiya at the foot of the mountain in winter. The name “Zao Mountains” derives from the {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} that were once worshipped at both shrines. === Location === It is said that the shrine was originally built atop a boundary mound between Ōshū and Ushū on Mt. Kumano, but because it was close to the crater and frequently burned due to eruptions, it was relocated around the {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} era (1645–1648) to the Omuro (Komedō) area of Mt. Katta after consultation between the Sendai Domain lord and the Matsudaira clan of Yamagata Zao Town History Compilation Committee, “Materials on Zao Mountain Worship,” *Zao Town History: Source Materials I*, 1987. Since ancient times, three shrines—Zao, {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|WD=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumano Shrine|Q3200355|WD=Q3200355|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano shrine}}—were enshrined on Mt. Kumano (elevation 1,841 m), the highest peak in the Zao range, and their village shrines still exist on both the Ōshū and Ushū sides. Along the main pilgrimage route (Miyagi side), the {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gates were erected as follows: the third torii at the Ōtorii enclosure in Maruta, Zao Town; the second torii at Iboishi; and the first torii at Fudō Falls. According to the documents of Sanjō-in at the Hōzawa route, the ritual method of worshipping the three shrines represents the correct ancient mountain practice . In {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 40), in the document “Shrine Survey Report” submitted by Kattamine Shrine (classified as a village shrine) included in *Survey of Shrines and Temples: Sendai City, Katta District, Shibata District, Volume 1 of 9* (held by the Miyagi Prefectural Archives), the shrine’s location is recorded as “enshrined at Kuraishi-dake, Miyamura, Katta District.” The report is dated July 4, Meiji 39, and lists Shrine Administrator Fujimoto Kunio and parish representatives from Miyamura, Katta District (present-day {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}}), with some residing in Sendai City. On the other hand, the {{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} Board of Education states that the shrine is located in {{ill|lt=Shichikashuku Town|Q1204398|WD=Q1204398|1=Shichikashuku, Miyagi}}, Katta District {{cite web|url=https://www.dokitan.com/column/02.html|title=Dokitan.com > History Column > No. 2 Kattamine Shrine (1)|publisher=Zao Town Board of Education|date=2009-05-03|accessdate=2025-07-06}}. === History === The shrine is identified as “Kattamine Shrine (Myōjin Taisha)” in the Katta District entry of the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}* (*Engishiki*, Volumes 9–10, {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5 [927]). The *{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}* records its establishment in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 1 (901). In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 11 (869), the year of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jōgan tsunami|1=UNKNOWN}} , the deity “Kattamine-no-Kami,” ranked Junior Ninth Rank, was promoted by extraordinary advancement to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade. {{ill|lt=Sakuma Dōgan|Q6562843|WD=Q6562843|1=Sakuma Dōgan}} remarked “How sudden indeed,” suggesting that some records may be missing. {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|WD=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}} interpreted the Jōgan tsunami as a “divine calamity caused by volcanic awe,” viewing it as the “wrath of the mountain god” as understood by the imperial court. In contrast, the “Shrine Survey Report” cited above quotes from the *{{ill|lt=Ruijū Kokushi|Q1038639|WD=Q1038639|1=Ruijū Kokushi}}* (compiled {{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 4 [892]), a rearrangement of the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, stating that the rank promotion was due to considerations of harvest conditions and drought or flood, without mentioning the tsunami. According to *Kinpu Sanzan Goengi*, the Zao Daigongen enshrined on Mt. Zao (Mt. Fubo) was opened by {{ill|lt=En no Ozunu|Q198946|WD=Q198946|1=En no Gyōja}} in {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} 8 (679). It is also said to have been invited by his uncle Gangyō. The Zao Daigongen Shrine long supervised the ascetics of the Zao region, managed by Gankyō-ji Temple at the eastern foot of Ōkattayama (Aomayama). *Kinpusan Kai-en narabi ni Higashidake Onsen Raireki* records that it was later revived by the monk Jitsugan, a spiritual descendant of {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}}. By the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} (late {{ill|lt=12th century|Q7061|WD=Q7061|1=12th century}}), the shrine prospered under the patronage of the {{ill|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara clan|Q1050942|WD=Q1050942|1=Northern Fujiwara}}, with Gankyō-ji expanding to 48 sub-temples. After the clan’s fall, decline and destruction by warfare led to ruin, and following the {{ill|lt=Ōnin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}, only three temples remained: Sannobō, Miyamotobō, and Takenobō Tanabe Marefumi, *Fūnai Fudoki*, 1772. Sannobō later fell into ruin around the {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} era. Miyamotobō became Kyūrenzō-ji during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1573–1592), when Lord {{ill|lt=Date Masamune|Q311183|WD=Q311183|1=Date Masamune}} granted it the mountain and temple name “Hōchizan Renzō-ji” and donated 200 koku of land. By longstanding precedent, Renzō-ji assumed administrative control over the shrine. Takenobō, known as Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō, oversaw the main pilgrimage route. During the {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}} era of the {{ill|lt=Northern Court|Q1201819|WD=Q1201819|1=Northern Court}} (1368–1374), three Tendai ascetic offices—Sanjō-in, Anraku-in, and Kakuzembō—were established at different approaches, collectively called the Ushū Three Bettō, all operating under Takenobō’s direction. In the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, as mountain pilgrimage flourished, they guided many worshippers to the summit shrine, though this prosperity later led to disputes over rights, culminating in lawsuits during the {{ill|lt=Ka’ei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} era (1848–1855). The 1907 *Shrine Survey* notes that the place name “Kattadake” was of relatively modern origin, with the mountain historically called Fubōsan or Zaodake, also known as Kuraishidake. It explains that because the shrine was enshrined on the summit of Ōkattayama, the divine name “Kattamine Shrine” was adopted, though confusion over the name remains. With the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} and enforcement of the Shinto–Buddhist separation edicts, “Zao Gongen” was adopted as a Shinto divine name in Yoshino, and monks became Shinto priests. Following this, in July {{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720|1=1869}}, the name was changed from “Zao Daigongen” to “Zao Ōkami,” and the shrine was called “Zao Shrine.” In September the same year, based on the interpretation that “Zao Ōkami” referred to the two deities {{ill|lt=Mikumari-no-Kami|Q11129547|WD=Q11129547|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami and Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|1=Mikumari}}, the shrine was renamed “Mikumari Shrine.” By 1875, it came to be called “Kattamine Shrine.” === Seasonal Relocation === A map of the Togatta residence created by the Katakura family around the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} era does not show a “Zao Daigongen Temporary Shrine,” instead marking “Takenobō” at the current Satomiya location. *Ōu Kanshiki Monrōshi* (1719) records that a shrine of Zao Gongen stood on the summit, with torii gates along the eastern mountain paths. April 8 was called the “Opening,” when snow melted and roads opened, while October 8 marked the “Closing.” During winter, villagers held rites with steamed rice and cloudy sake. *An’ei Fudoki Goyōshodashi* (1777) records a “Zao Gongen Temporary Shrine,” noting that due to heavy snow, the deity was relocated downhill on October 8 and returned on April 8. The temporary shrine stood at the same location as Takenobō, indicating a close relationship. Today, relocation between the Satomiya and Okumiya coincides with the opening of the Zao Echo Line and {{ill|lt=Zao Highline|Q11621931|WD=Q11621931|1=Zao High Line}} roads, typically in late April, though timing varies by snowfall. Before relocation, {{ill|lt=Ōsaki Hachimangū|Q874428|WD=Q874428|1=Ōsaki Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|WD=Q46747|1=Sendai}} performs annual snow-clearing service to expose the shrine buildings and approach paths. === Access === [[File:Kattamine shrine winter.jpg|thumb|The shrine during severe winter when roads are closed
(Photo taken March 9, 2006)]] {{seealso|Okama (Zao Mountains)}} From the free Zao Echo Line, enter the Zao Highline (daytime toll road), park at the free Katta Pass parking lot in front of the Miyagi Prefectural Zao Rest House, then walk about 15 minutes. For winter closures, see {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture Route / Yamagata Prefecture Route 12 Shiroishi–Kaminoyama Line#Zao Echo Line|Q11453957|WD=Q11453957|1=Miyagi Prefectural Road and Yamagata Prefectural Road Route 12}}. {{-}} === Shrines with the Same Name === {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left; font-size:90%;" !colspan="6" |Location !rowspan="2" |Current name !rowspan="2" |Other name |- !{{ill|lt=Prefecture|Q50337|WD=Q50337|lt=Pref.|1=Prefectures of Japan}} !{{ill|lt=District|Q517267|WD=Q517267|1=Commandery (China)}} !{{ill|lt=Town|Q1059478|WD=Q1059478|1=Towns of Japan}} !colspan="2" |Area !Position |- |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} |rowspan="3" |{{ill|lt=Katta District|Q1131771|WD=Q1131771|1=Katta District, Miyagi}} | |rowspan="2" |Zao / Mt. Katta (relocated from Mt. Kumano) |Summit area |{{Coord|38|7|39.7|N|140|26|56.42|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Okumiya)}} !Kattamine Shrine |'''Okumiya''' |- |rowspan="2" |{{ill|lt=Zao Town|Q1347874|WD=Q1347874|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} |Eastern foothills / former Takenobō site in {{ill|lt=Togatta Onsen|Q11641981|WD=Q11641981|1=Togatta Onsen}} |{{Coord|38|7|28.61|N|140|34|35.57|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Satomiya)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Togatta Onsen, Zao Town)|Q11396255|WD=Q11396255|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Satomiya''' |- |{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aomayama|1=UNKNOWN}} |The mountain itself (Ōkattayama) enshrined together with Shiratori Shrine (Yamato Takeru) |{{Coord|38|3|1.1|N|140|39|11.7|E|region:JP|map|name=Kattamine Shrine (Shiratori Daimyōjin)}} !{{ill|lt=Kattamine Shrine (Miya, Zao Town)|Q11396254|WD=Q11396254|lt=Kattamine Shrine|1=Kattamine Shrine}} |'''Shiratori Daimyōjin''' |} {{Coordinate list|section=Shrines with the Same Name}} There are also shrines of the same name on the {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|WD=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} side: Zao Daigongen (Kattamine Shrine, Shimohōzawa, {{ill|lt=Yamagata City|Q205526|WD=Q205526|1=Yamagata (city)}}), Kattamine Shrine (Zao Hangō, Yamagata City), and Kattamine Shrine (Kanaya, {{ill|lt=Kaminoyama City|Q770187|WD=Q770187|1=Kaminoyama}}). === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Zao Mountain Shrine|Q11621941|WD=Q11621941|1=Zaōsan Shrine}} === External links === * {{cite web|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622222024/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|archivedate=2019-06-22|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/270801.html|title=Kattamine Shrine (Mutsu Province, Katta District)|publisher={{ill|lt=Kokugakuin University|Q495330|WD=Q495330|1=Kokugakuin University}}|accessdate=2025-07-06}} * [http://www.dokitan.com/index.html Official website “Dokitan.com” – History and Cultural Properties of Zao Town] (Zao Town Board of Education) * [http://www.zaodera.jp/ Kinpusan Zao-ji Temple] (A Shingon-affiliated independent temple sharing the historical name with the Edo-period “Kinpusan Zao-ji Takenobō,” with its main hall at Sai-no-Kawara along the Zao Echo Line and a branch temple in Togatta Onsen) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kattamine Shrine (Katta-dake)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kattamine Shrine (Katta-dake)]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] structures in Miyagi Prefecture]]
